questions.question: Explain the three main file allocation methods and state one advantage and one disadvantage of each.
keyPoints: Contiguous Allocation: Sequential blocks, fast access but suffers from external fragmentation
Linked Allocation: Blocks linked via pointers, no fragmentation but poor random access
Indexed Allocation: Uses index block, supports direct access but requires extra storage for index

questions.question: What is an inode? List at least five fields stored in an inode structure.
keyPoints: Inode (Index Node) stores file metadata in UNIX file systems
Fields include: file permissions/ownership, file size, timestamps (access/modify/change), block pointers, link count, file type

questions.question: Describe the bit vector method for free space management. What are its advantages and disadvantages?
keyPoints: Maintains bitmap with one bit per block (0=free, 1=allocated)
Advantages: Fast allocation decisions, simple implementation
Disadvantages: Requires extra memory for bitmap (larger disks need larger bitmaps), scanning for consecutive blocks can be slow

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Virtual File System (VFS) in operating systems? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: VFS provides abstraction layer between user applications and actual file systems
Allows multiple different file system types to coexist (ext4, NTFS, FAT, etc.)
Provides uniform system call interface regardless of underlying file system
Example: Users can mount USB (FAT32), hard disk (ext4), and network share (NFS) all accessible through same file operations

questions.question: Explain the concept of file system mounting. What information is read from the superblock during mounting?
keyPoints: Mounting attaches a file system to the directory tree at a mount point
Superblock contains: block size, free inode count, free block count, inode size, pointers to free inode/block lists
OS validates file system integrity before making it accessible

questions.question: Discuss the directory implementation in file systems. What operations must a directory data structure support?
keyPoints: Directory maps file names to their metadata/inode locations
Operations: create file, delete file, list contents, search for file, rename file
Implementation can be linear list, hash table, or B-tree
Must support efficient search and update operations

questions.question: What is the difference between internal fragmentation and external fragmentation in file systems?
keyPoints: Internal fragmentation: wasted space within allocated blocks due to block size being larger than file size
External fragmentation: scattered free blocks that cannot accommodate large files despite sufficient total free space
Contiguous allocation suffers from external, all methods may have internal fragmentation

questions.question: Describe the complete process that occurs when a user requests to open and read a file in a file system. Include the role of directory lookup, inode retrieval, buffer cache, and file descriptor table.
keyPoints: User calls open() system call with file path
Kernel searches directory structure to find file name and inode number
Inode is read from disk (or buffer cache) and validated for permissions
File descriptor entry created in open file table pointing to inode
For read(): kernel checks buffer cache for requested blocks
If not cached, issues disk I/O to read blocks
Data copied to user buffer, file position updated
File descriptor tracks current position for subsequent operations

questions.question: Compare contiguous, linked, and indexed file allocation methods in terms of: (i) External fragmentation (ii) Random access support (iii) Access time for sequential and direct access (iv) Storage overhead. Provide a summary table.
keyPoints: Contiguous: High external fragmentation, excellent random access, O(1) direct access, O(n) sequential, minimum overhead (just start block + length)
Linked: No external fragmentation, no random access, O(n) for both, pointer overhead per block
Indexed: No external fragmentation, excellent random access, O(1) direct access, O(1) sequential, needs extra index block per file
Summary shows trade-offs between flexibility, performance, and storage efficiency

questions.question: Explain four different methods of free space management in file systems. For each method, describe how allocation and deallocation are performed and mention its advantages and limitations.
keyPoints: Bit Vector: Bitmap tracks free blocks, allocation finds first 0, deallocation sets bit to 0. Fast but needs memory
Linked List: Free blocks linked together, allocation removes from list, deallocation adds to list. Simple but slow traversal
Grouping: First free block stores addresses of other free blocks. Fast allocation of multiple blocks
Counting: Stores starting block and count of consecutive free blocks. Efficient for large contiguous allocations
Each method balances memory usage, allocation speed, and implementation complexity
